terms.usage_note: The process by which human beings participate in the divine nature. Central concept in Eastern Orthodox theology. Preserve as 'theosis' in contexts where the technical meaning is essential; render as 'divinization' in explanatory contexts.

Not mere intellectual knowledge but direct, experiential knowing of divine reality. In Gnostic contexts, preserve as 'gnosis' untranslated. In patristic contexts where the author uses it neutrally, render as 'knowledge' with Greek in parentheses on first use.

Meaning shifted dramatically between 3rd and 5th centuries. In pre-Nicene usage often means 'substance' or 'underlying reality' (close to ousia). In post-Nicene Trinitarian usage means 'person' (the three hypostases of the Trinity). Flag for review in every instance — context determines rendering.

The shared divine essence in Trinitarian theology (one ousia, three hypostases). In philosophical contexts can mean 'being' or 'substance' more broadly. Render as 'essence' in theological contexts, 'being' in philosophical.

When referring to Christ (as in John 1:1), capitalize as 'Word' and preserve 'Logos' in parentheses. In philosophical contexts (Stoic, Neoplatonic), render as 'reason' or 'rational principle'. In rhetorical contexts, 'speech' or 'argument'.

Capitalize as 'Spirit' when clearly referring to the Holy Spirit. Lowercase 'spirit' for the human spirit or spiritual nature. The root meaning 'breath' is sometimes relevant — note when the author seems to be playing on the dual meaning.
In Neoplatonic contexts (and in Valentinian Gnosticism where Nous is an aeon), preserve untranslated. In general patristic usage, render as 'mind' or 'intellect'.

In Gnostic contexts (the divine Pleroma, the totality of divine emanations), always preserve untranslated as 'Pleroma' capitalized. In Pauline/orthodox usage (e.g., Colossians 1:19), render as 'fullness' with Greek in parentheses.
When referring to the Gnostic aeon Sophia, preserve untranslated and capitalized. When used as a common noun meaning 'wisdom', translate normally.

In Gnostic contexts, always preserve as 'Demiurge' capitalized — it refers to the inferior creator god. In Platonic philosophical contexts (Timaeus), can be rendered as 'craftsman' or 'creator' with Greek in parentheses.
In Gnostic contexts (the archons who rule the material world), preserve as 'archon(s)'. In political/historical contexts, render as 'ruler' or 'magistrate'.
The Gnostic term for the fragment of divine light within human beings. Always render as 'spark' with Greek in parentheses on first use. This is a central concept for the Theosis Library project.
In Gnostic contexts (divine emanations from the Pleroma), preserve as 'aeon(s)'. In temporal contexts, render as 'age' or 'eternity'.

Traditionally rendered 'only-begotten' in reference to Christ. Modern scholarship debates whether it means 'only-begotten' or simply 'unique/one of a kind'. Flag for the translator's attention in every instance — this term is directly relevant to the project's central question.
